original_abstract:
In recent decades, Practical Theology has undergone a significant shift toward empirical investigation, moving beyond the mere application of doctrine to include the systematic study of Christian practices. At North-West University, this trend is clearly reflected in postgraduate research, where nearly all recent studies incorporate empirical components. However, a quantitative and qualitative review of research outputs between 2010 and 2013 suggests that while empirical methods are prevalent, there is often a lack of rigorous, transparent methodology regarding data analysis and coding. Many researchers appear to approach these phases intuitively rather than systematically, potentially undermining the credibility and "truth value" of their findings.

To address this gap, this article provides an overview of essential coding techniques—such as in vivo, process, and axial coding—and evaluates various established approaches to qualitative data analysis, including those of Tesch and Creswell. By integrating these perspectives, the author proposes a five-phase model specifically tailored for practical theological studies: preparing the data, coding, member checking, interpreting results, and presenting the data. The implementation of this systematic framework aims to enhance the empirical research capacity of both students and lecturers, ensuring that qualitative findings are robust, credible, and academically rigorous.
humanized_abstract:
While reflection on the past 30 years of developments in Practical Theology does indicate a clear emphasis on empirical investigations and a more scientific approach to theology in general, after 20 centuries of reflection on Christian practices one would expect a more systematic approach to these practices. An empirical survey of postgraduate research done at North-West University indeed supports this assumption with almost all studies completed since 2010 including empirical research components. A quantitative and qualitative analysis of empirical research done at the university in the period 2010-2013, reveals on the one hand that empirical research components were adequately dealt with in almost all cases. On the other hand, however, a closer look into the procedures used in the analysis of the empirical data and in the coding of this data reveals that researches deal with these aspects in an unscientific way. Almost without exception they make use of ad hoc and seemingly rather arbitrary procedures in the analysis of the data, as well as in the coding of it. While empirical methods are used meticulously and successfully in the initial phases of empirical research, the data-analysis and -coding are dealt with in a sloppy and unscientific manner, which could jeopardize the value and even the truth value of the research as a whole.

The absence of formal training in qualitative research methods for students as well as lecturers in the Faculty of Theology and Religion (FTR) at the University of Pretoria has created a void in empirical research skills. In an effort to address this gap, this article provides an overview of basic coding techniques namely in vivo, process and axial coding and critically examined established qualitative data analysis methods in light of the works of Tesch and Creswell. A five-phase research methodology and design is proposed and applied to empirical practical theological studies. The phases include preparing the data; coding; member checking; interpretation of results and presentation of data. This structured approach is intended to establish empirical research skills in students and lecturers of the FTR. It is hoped that this will enhance the qualitative research capacity in the Faculty.
